title: practical english usage - Part 356
content:
These sort of cars are enormously expensive to run.

Plural demonstratives (these and those) can also be used.

I'm interested in any new kind of developments . ..

especially in an informal style.

Singular sort of, kind of and type of can also be followed by plural nouns, I'm interested in any new type of development in computer science.

This sort of car is enormously expensive to run.

or type of followed by a singular noun.

When  we  are talking about one sort of thing, we  can use sort of, kind of 2 singular      and   plural;    these      sort     of,   etc vocabulary' •  595  still, yet and already: time She's still asleep. Is it still raining?                   -> Still is used to say that something has, perhaps surprisingly, not finished.

it might have happened.

- already is used to say that something has happened early, or earlier than - yet is used to talk about something that is expected - still is used to say that something is continuing and has not stopped or expected, around the present. Briefly: Still, yet and already can all be used to talk about things which  are going on, 1  meanings 595  still,    yet    and     already:       time      __ ►     398 and start         begin You're talking complete nonsense, as usual.

Talk is used before sense, nonsense and other words with similar meanings.

Hello. Could I speak to Karen, please?

One usually asks to speak to somebody on the phone (AmE also speak with).

His throat operation has left him  unable to speak.

We spoke Dutch so that the children wouldn't understand.

She speaks three languages fluently.

and to the physical ability to speak.

Speak is the usual word to refer to knowledge and use of languages, 4 languages The Pope spoke to the crowd for seventy minutes about world peace.

developments in low-temperature physics.

This is Professor Rosalind Bowen, who is going to speak to us on recent This is Patrick Allen, who's going to talk to us about gardening.

preferred for more formal lectures, sermons, etc. Compare: Talk is often used for the act of giving an informal lecture (a talk); speak is 3   lectures,      etc After she had finished explaining her plans, nobody spoke.

They had a fight last week, and now they're not speaking to one another.

I'll have to speak to that boy - he's getting very lazy.

Speak is often used for communication in more serious or formal situations.

Could I talk to you about my plans for a few minutes?

When she walked into the room everybody stopped talking.

Talk is the more usual word for informal communication.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  595  still, yet and already: time are common with already and yet; Americans often use past tenses. Compare: Various tenses are possible with all three words. In British English, perfect tenses 7 tenses Is my coat dry already? That was quick!

- Is my coat dry yet?

(= I think you’ ve probably met him.) Have you already met Professor Hawkins?

(= ɪ  don't know whether you've met him.) - Have you met Professor Hawkins yet?

Questions with already often suggest that something has happened. Compare: 6 yet    or  already        in  questions I can't speak French yet, but I hope I will be able to soon.

- I still can't speak Russian, after all these years of study.

hoping that she will get one.) She hasn't got a job yet. (Looking forward: she hasn't got a job now, but we're and this situation is continuing.) - She still hasn't got a job. (Looking back: she hasn't had a job for a long time, Compare: Still not looks back towards the past; not yet looks towards the future.

5 still    not    or  not    yet?

nut                   u h h (    Already 4 *  I was fourteen . . .) When I was fourteen I already knew that I wanted to be a doctor.

We   do not usually put already before time expressions.

Are you here already? You must have run all the way.

go at the end of a clause, for emphasis.

Already usually goes with the verb, in 'mid-position' (►   200). It can also Have you already finished? That was quick!

‘ You must go to Scotland.’ ‘ I’ve already been there.’ ‘ When’s Sophie going to come? ‘ She's already here.’ or earlier than it might have happened.
